speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is roll seven for Judith Rubinstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my maternal grandparents, Leo and Fegie Leah Hofstetter. And that picture was taken on their youngest daughter, the eleventh child's wedding. And they seem to be very pleased-- they married off the youngest. Unfortunately, they were taken to Auschwitz, and they were both killed. Grandpa was very pessimistic. He knew that's nothing good coming out from this war. But unfortunately, he couldn't do anything about it. The picture was taken approximately 1930. I think. This picture was taken at my youngest aunt, Chana's, wedding. And my mother is in the far left, and my grandma and three of my aunts. And the far right is a cousin of mine-- he's the only surviving member from this picture. He lives here in Toronto. He's in medicine. The three girls, two, I met in Auschwitz. They were at that time 18, 19, and 16. I-- I met them once. And unfortunately, they never made it. I never heard of them again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The picture was taken before the war, in the town of Mezocsat, where my mother was born and her whole family lived. It was a happy occasion to gather the clan. The picture is my Aunt Chana, my mother's youngest sister, with her little daughter Judith. The picture was taken just shortly before they were taken to Auschwitz with grandpa and grandma. And since she was a very good-looking, strong, beautiful woman, Mengele decided to send her to work, and separated from her little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she, somehow, she felt that it's not right. She started to cry that she's not going to leave her daughter. So Mengele said, OK, then you'll go with your daughter. And let her go to the left. And she was killed, with her daughter and grandma instantly.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two gentlemen in that picture were working as translators in the American army. One were from New York, the other one from Texas. And just by sheer coincidence, we met them. And they were so nice to us, became almost like a father. They brought us food and clothes, and whatever we needed-- medicine. And promised to each other that we will meet again. Unfortunately, I lost their address. And one I met-- I wrote to one, a Mr. Suffield on the right, who lives in-- who lived in New York-- I don't know if he's still alive. And he never answered me. I was busy in the first few years just to-- we worry about make a living. I had no time to-- and my English was very poor at that time. So I just let it go. And now it's unfortunately, it's too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in 1945, right after liberation. They are drinking for the liberation. That was the day when that part of Germany became free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's on the way on, it was in Schwelm-- in the city of Schwelm in Westphalia, close to the Baltic Sea. This picture was taken after the liberation, provided by the American soldiers, our good friends, Mr. Suffield and Mr. Cooper. We-- the clothes what we have on then was, well, tatters-- we really looked horrible. So they went into the SS army barrack, and got me a lot of bed sheets, blue and white bed sheets. Well, I still didn't have a sewing machine. So they went out and they find me an old sewing machine, and I was in business. I sewed all these dresses for the girls, because they wanted to look a little bit more presentable. By that time our hair grew. And, as this picture shows, that we were quite a happy bunch-- we being happy to alive. I am the only one from Hungary. The others are all from Czechoslovakia. Greta, on the right, she find her fiance after the war. Selma, the next one, went to England, find her fiance. Klara lives in Israel. The next one, Magda, is in Australia. And the left, that's me. Here in this right, Regina, is my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left-- left. It's your left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on the left. On the left is Regina, she lives in Israel. And then the two twins, Anetchka, in the middle, she somehow disappeared in-- in-- in Czechoslovakia. And the two girls are-- I don't know exactly where it is. And the last one, Margit Singer, she find her mother-- unbelievable, but she was still alive, an old lady. But she was just too sick for the Nazis to carry her out from her home. And she made it, survived. And when we came back, she-- it was just too much happiness for her. And she couldn't take it, had a weak heart, and two days later, she died. But she saw her daughter before she died. So this is a precious picture of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So we'll just do it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this picture was taken after liberation by our good friends, by the American soldiers. We had no decent clothes to wear. And they got us, from the Nazi headquarters, they got us the blue and white checkered bed sheets. And they got me a sewing machine, and I was in business, sewing for all the girls, the clothes. And that's why we look like we are wearing a uniform. Greta in the far left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Top left.

speaker: interviewee
text: The top left, lives in Israel. She find her fiance right after the war. The next one is Selma-- she lives in England. She finds her fiance there. Then the third one is Klara-- she lives in Israel. I don't know if she got married. And next, Magda lives in Australia. Then it's me. In the bottom left is Regina, is my cousin, whom I find in Auschwitz. She is-- she lives in Ramla, in Israel. The-- the next one, and the second next, and they twins. They live somewhere in America I think. And Anetchka, she was a real Czech, she didn't speak Hungarian. She disappeared-- lives, if she's alive, she's somewhere in Czechoslovakia. And the last one is Margit Singer, who find her mother after liberation. We arrived to Bratislava, and she find out in a railway station that-- we thought it's just a rumor that an old lady is still alive. And surely, she was alive. She was too sick for the police to carry her away, and she lived just long enough to see her daughter. And she died about two or three days later. This is my pride of joy. This is my entire family. My son and daughter with their children. At the far left, in the-- in the back, is Tamal-- we just married a month ago. And then my son, Robert Eli. He was trained in philosophy. Then the daughter, wife, Renee. Next one is Ilan, who is in-- now is a big boy. He's in high school. Rochelle, our daughter, who is an artist. Her little girl, Alicia. And our son-in-law, Alan Kaplan, who is a psychiatry. And then second row, grandma and grandpa. And then in the bottom, in the left, is Jesse. He's 12 now. Sig, who's 10. And Hadassah, she is eight. And Eras, in the far right, is just 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just also explain who grandma and grandpa are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, grandma and grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they? Where are they seated? Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle. I mentioned that, I think. They're in the middle, grandpa and grandma, no? OK. This picture is my pride and joy-- my whole entire family. My son and daughters' children. In the left is Tamal-- young lady, who just married recently. Then my son, Robert Eli, who was trained as a philosopher. And then wife, Renee-- a good girl. Ilan, who is in high school. Then Rochelle, our daughter. She's an artist. Her little girl, Alicia. And Alan Kaplan, our son-in-law-- he's in psychiatry. Then here-- there we're sitting, me and my husband, grandpa and grandma-- beaming, because we are very happy, we were happy on this occasion. Just tell me your name, for grandma, and the name of your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my name Judy Rubinstein, and my husband, Bill Rubinstein-- the proud grandparents. And then in the lower part is Jesse on the left. He is 12. And Sigi, he's going to be 10. And little Haddassah, who's eight. And Eras, in the far right, he's just turned 13. And this is my entire family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, you behave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone looking up here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep on smiling. This little group is part of our immediate family. My husband, next to me, Bill. And our second daughter, Renee, our son's wife, who is-- became really a second daughter to us. Our son is away in Israel-- I can't introduce him. And then on the left is Ilan, the big boy Ilan, who is finishing high school this year. And Tamal, the young lady, who just got married a month ago. And then little-- little Haddasah-- who is not so little anymore. And she is top student, and is a smiley one. And here's Eras-- my pride of joy-- Eras Rubinstein, who is a good student, just turned 13. And we have high hopes for him. My daughter and his-- her family, unfortunately, couldn't be here. [NON-ENGLISH]
